mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Pakistan on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the region called South Asia. Pakistan sits between India, China, Afghanistan and Iran 🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Pakistan?
mapQuestions.answer: Islamabad. It is a planned modern city, built in the 1960s next to the older city of Rawalpindi, at the foot of the Margalla Hills.

mapQuestions.question: What ancient river runs through the whole country?
mapQuestions.answer: The Indus. It is one of the world's great rivers, flowing 3,180 km from Tibet down through Pakistan to the Arabian Sea. The whole country was named after it.

mapQuestions.question: What is the second-highest mountain in the world?
mapQuestions.answer: K2, 8,611 metres, in the Karakoram range in northern Pakistan. K2 is even more deadly than Everest — about 25% of climbers who try the summit die.

mapQuestions.question: How many people live in Pakistan?
mapQuestions.answer: About 240 million — the 5th most populous country in the world, more than Germany, France, UK and Italy combined.

foods.name: Biryani
foods.description: Long-grain basmati rice layered with spiced meat (lamb, chicken or beef), saffron, onions, yogurt and herbs. Every region has its version — Sindhi, Lahori, Karachi.

foods.name: Nihari
foods.description: A slow-cooked beef stew, simmered overnight until the meat falls apart. Heavy with bone-marrow flavour. Eaten for breakfast with fresh naan, especially on weekends.

foods.name: Karahi
foods.description: Meat (usually chicken or mutton) cooked in a wok-like pan called a karahi, with fresh tomatoes, ginger, green chillies and garam masala. Eaten torn-and-scoop with hot naan.

foods.name: Naan + roti
foods.description: Naan: a leavened flatbread baked in a clay tandoor oven until puffed and golden. Roti: a smaller, unleavened daily flatbread cooked on a tava. Used as a spoon for everything else.

foods.name: Samosas
foods.description: A folded triangle of crisp pastry stuffed with spiced potato and peas, or sometimes mince. Eaten as a snack with green chutney and hot sweet milky chai.

foods.name: Chai
foods.description: Strong black tea boiled with milk, cardamom, sometimes ginger or cloves, and a lot of sugar. Drunk all day, every day. Pakistanis are some of the world's biggest tea-drinkers.

scavengerItems.label: A bright "truck art" painted lorry, decorated wing to wing
A mosque with a turquoise or white dome and a tall minaret
Kids playing cricket in a street, on a dirt lot or in a park
A man in shalwar kameez (loose tunic and trousers)
A woman in a colourful dupatta scarf
A flame inside a tandoor oven baking naan
A roadside chaiwala (tea stall) with a big steaming pot
A pile of Pakistani mangoes (Anwar Ratol or Chaunsa varieties are legendary)
The Karakoram or Hindu Kush mountains in the far north
A qawwali music performance, or speakers playing qawwali in a shop
A Pashtun pakol (flat wool cap) on someone's head

geoMatches.description: The capital. Modern, planned, leafy and quiet by Pakistani standards. The Margalla Hills rise right next to the city, with monkeys on the roadside.
The cultural capital. Mughal monuments (Badshahi Mosque, Lahore Fort), legendary food, walled old city, and the festival of Basant kites in spring.
The biggest city, on the Arabian Sea. Pakistan's economic centre. Sprawling, chaotic, with the world's biggest open-air bazaar, fish markets, and a 50-mile coastline.
A 2,000-year-old city in the north-west, near the Khyber Pass into Afghanistan. Pashtun heartland, hot grilled meat, ancient mosques and bazaars.
A high mountain valley in the Karakoram. Apricot orchards, glacier views, ancient forts. One of the most beautiful places on Earth, according to many travellers.
The ruins of a 5,000-year-old city in southern Pakistan. Part of the Indus Valley Civilization, with grid streets, brick houses, a public bath and a working sewer system.

senses.smell: Cumin and cardamom toasting, smoke from tandoor ovens, chai brewing on a roadside, jasmine in courtyards, hot cooking oil on samosas, cold mountain air in the north…
senses.hear: The call to prayer five times a day, "Salam aleikum", cricket commentary in Urdu, car horns (constant in cities), qawwali music with hand-clapping, drums and harmonium…
senses.taste: Hot spicy biryani, sweet milky chai, smoky tandoor naan, juicy ripe Pakistani mango (the best in the world, some Pakistanis will tell you), sweet sticky jalebi, sharp green chutney…
senses.feel: 40°C+ Punjab heat in summer, cool mountain air in the north, soft cotton shalwar kameez, the warmth of a chai cup in winter, sticky sugar on fingers from jalebi…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How old is civilisation in Pakistan?
stories.body: Pakistan is home to one of the world's oldest civilisations — the Indus Valley Civilization, which flourished about 5,000 years ago, around the same time as ancient Egypt. They built planned cities like Mohenjo-daro and Harappa, with grid streets, brick houses, a working sewer system, public baths and granaries. They had a written language that still has not been fully deciphered, and a peaceful society — almost no weapons have been found in their cities. Then around 1900 BCE, something happened (drought, the river changing course, possibly invasion) and the cities were abandoned. Modern Pakistanis live where their distant ancestors built one of humanity's first urban civilisations.

stories.question: Why does Pakistan have so many huge mountains?
stories.body: Pakistan has 5 of the world's 14 mountains over 8,000 metres tall. The most famous is K2 (8,611m), the second-highest mountain in the world after Everest. K2 is much more dangerous than Everest: about 25% of climbers who try the summit die. The Karakoram range also has the longest non-polar glacier on Earth, the Siachen Glacier, where Pakistani and Indian soldiers have stared at each other across an icy mountainside for decades over disputed land. Pakistanis love their mountains. The Hunza Valley, with apricot orchards under glacier-fed peaks, is considered by many to be one of the most beautiful places on Earth.

stories.location: 📍 Anywhere a cricket match is on
stories.question: Why is cricket basically a religion in Pakistan?
stories.body: Pakistan is one of the world's top cricket countries. It won the 1992 Cricket World Cup. The team's biggest hero was Imran Khan, who captained the winning team and later became Prime Minister. Cricket is played in every street, on every patch of dirt, in alleys between cars. When Pakistan plays India (its arch rival), the streets empty: everyone is watching. The two countries have been to war four times since they became independent, but they always meet on the cricket pitch. A Pakistan-vs-India match is one of the most-watched sporting events on Earth, every time.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Two countries can fight wars and still happily play sport against each other. What does that tell you about people?

stories.question: How did Pakistan become a country?
stories.body: When the British finally left India in 1947 after almost 200 years of colonial rule, the country was split into two: Hindu-majority India and Muslim-majority Pakistan. The split was rushed — a British lawyer named Cyril Radcliffe was given just five weeks to draw the new border, with no real expertise in the area. The result was one of the largest forced migrations in human history. Around 15 million people fled in both directions — Muslims moving to Pakistan, Hindus and Sikhs moving to India. Up to two million people died in the violence. Many Pakistani families today still have grandparents who arrived from India with nothing in 1947. The border between India and Pakistan is still one of the most heavily guarded in the world.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A line drawn on a map by a stranger uprooted 15 million people. Could there have been a better way to split India?

stories.question: What is Pakistan now?
stories.body: Pakistan is one of the youngest big countries in the world (only 78 years old, created in 1947) but sits on top of one of the oldest civilisations on Earth. About 240 million people live there — more than Germany, France, the UK and Italy put together. Pakistan has nuclear weapons (since 1998), one of only nine countries that do. It has produced two female heads of state (Benazir Bhutto, twice Prime Minister) and the Nobel Peace Prize winner Malala Yousafzai, who was shot by the Taliban as a teenager for going to school, recovered, and now campaigns for girls' education worldwide. Pakistan faces huge challenges: climate change has caused devastating floods, the economy is unstable, and politics is rough. But it is also one of the world's great food cultures, with some of the warmest hospitality you will ever experience anywhere.

convoQuestions.question: Best Pakistani food you have eaten so far?
Have you seen "truck art" up close? What was the wildest design?
How spicy is the food really? Survival rating out of 10?
Have you been to a chai stall yet? How many cups of chai is too many in one day?
Mountains, cities, deserts, beach — which side of Pakistan would you most want to return to?
Pakistan is younger than your grandparents but sits on a 5,000-year-old civilisation. How does it feel to walk somewhere that ancient?

wordSearch.hint: Pakistani foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Working buffalo or zebu
animals.description: Ploughing fields, pulling carts, providing milk for chai across Punjab and Sindh.

animals.name: Black kite
animals.description: Forked-tail bird of prey circling above every Pakistani city, especially Karachi and Lahore.

animals.name: Bazaar cat
animals.description: Sleek street cats in every old-city market, fed by spice traders.

animals.name: Markhor
animals.description: Pakistan's national animal — a wild goat with massive corkscrew horns. In Chitral and Hunza.

animals.name: Snow leopard
animals.description: In the high Karakoram — fewer than 250 in the country. Spotted by very few people.

animals.name: Indus river dolphin
animals.description: A blind freshwater dolphin found only in the Indus. Critically endangered.
